nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC) is designed to promote research on abstract reasoning, a fundamental aspect of human intelligence.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Common approaches to ARC treat it as a language-oriented problem, addressed by large language models (LLMs) or recurrent reasoning models.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the puzzle-like tasks in ARC are inherently visual

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: existing research has rarely approached the problem from a vision-centric perspective.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: formulate ARC within a vision paradigm, framing it as an image-to-image translation problem.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: represent the inputs on a “canvas" that can be processed like natural images.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: To incorporate visual priors

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: apply standard vision architectures, such as a vanilla Vision Transformer (ViT), to perform image-to-image mapping.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Our model is trained from scratch solely on ARC data

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: generalizes to unseen tasks through test-time training.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Our framework, termed Vision ARC (VARC)

nodes.label: pattern

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: ARC consists of a collection of puzzle-like tasks (Fig. 1, top), each containing only a few examples governed by a unique underlying transformation rule.

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: The model is expected to make predictions on each unseen task given a few examples.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: humans are capable of solving various ARC tasks [25, 31, 32]

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the benchmark remains highly challenging for today's leading machine learning systems [44, 42].

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: methods based on large language models (LLMs)

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: have proven highly competitive.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: convert ARC inputs into sequences of text tokens for language modeling.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The LLMs are pre-trained on internet-scale data

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: from which they learn transferable common sense.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: recurrent models [53, 27]

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: has achieved impressive results on ARC

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: without relying on internet-scale data.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: These models are trained from scratch on ARC data only

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: perform inference through recurrent, iterative reasoning.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: these recurrent models draw strong inspiration from the success of language modeling.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the ARC puzzles are typically presented visually

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: existing research has rarely framed ARC as a vision-centric problem.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: many concepts in ARC are inherently visual and physical: e.g., reflection, symmetry, and gravity, as shown in Fig. 1.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: Humans can solve these tasks not merely from the demonstrations, but by reasoning through analogy to their common sense obtained from external experience.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: Such common sense can be acquired through observing the world, particularly, the visual world.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: approach ARC from a vision-centric perspective.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: frame each puzzle as an image-to-image translation problem.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: Abstraction and inference can arise directly from visual learning, without explicit linguistic intermediates.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: This perspective connects ARC to classical image-to-image problems, ranging from low-level image processing (e.g., [16, 43]) to high-level image understanding (e.g., [38, 46]).

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: apply standard vision models (e.g., Vision Transformers [17] or convolutional networks [30]) to tackle the ARC problem.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: incorporating visual priors is crucial.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: These priors include 2D spatial locality, translation invariance, and scale invariance.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: represent the inputs on a "canvas" with flexible geometric transformations

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: To facilitate learning these priors, allowing the inputs to be processed as if they were natural images.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: A patch on the canvas can consist of exponentially many color combinations, which helps reduce overfitting and encourages the model to learn spatial priors rather than merely memorize.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: train our model from scratch using ARC-only data.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: perform test-time training [9, 24, 49, 1, 53, 27] to adapt the model to the task

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: enabling it to generalize from only a few examples.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Our framework, termed Vision ARC (VARC)

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: shows strong performance on the ARC benchmarks (e.g., Fig. 2).

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: VARC

nodes.label: pattern

nodes.label: pattern

nodes.label: pattern

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: matching the reported average human performance [31] on the ARC-1 dataset.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the design of the ARC benchmark is based on human observations and induced rules abstracted from the visual and physical world.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: It is natural to explore vision-driven approaches for ARC.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: human reasoning is not confined to language or vision in isolation, but instead should integrate information across modalities.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The ARC benchmark (top) consists of a collection of many different tasks, where each task has a few (e.g., 2-4) examples.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: propose the Vision ARC (VARC) framework, which addresses the ARC problem as an image-to-image translation problem, from a computer vision perspective (bottom).

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: These concepts are closely related to the visual and physical world.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: classical visual reasoning protocols generally involve a training set and a test set, both of which can be viewed as instances of the same task.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: ARC consists of a large collection of distinct tasks, each defined by only a few examples.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the "few-shot, many-task" nature of ARC

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: LLMs have been regarded as a natural solution.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: A new task can be converted into a sequence of tokens, treated as a prompt, and processed by LLMs via in-context few-shot learning [55, 10].

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: recurrent models [53, 27]

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: have been proven effective for ARC

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: without the requirement of internet-scale pre-training.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: These models aim to mimic the hierarchical and multi-timescale processing of the human brain [53] for reasoning.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: adopt test-time training [9, 24, 49] on the few demonstration examples.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the ViT-ARC method [34] attempts to address the ARC problem using vision models.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: has only shown the ability to fit individual tasks in the training set

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: it is unable to generalize or solve any unseen test task.

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: the ARC protocol, whose essence lies precisely in few-shot, cross-task generalization.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: our framework is designed to address the "few-shot, many-task" nature of ARC.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: VARC

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: correctly solves these challenging tasks.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The ARC benchmark consists of several hundred very few-shot (e.g., 2 to 4-shot) reasoning tasks.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Each task, denoted by T, involves a unique underlying transformation rule, mapping from an input x to an output y.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: x and y are both 2D grids with maximum size 30×30, in which each location has one of C different color indexes (e.g., C=10).

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Each task includes a few demonstration examples.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: For a demonstration pair (x, y), both x and y are known to the model.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: demonstration set of task T as: Demo={(xi, Yi)}=1, where m is the number of pairs (e.g., m is 2 to 4).

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Each task T also contains a few inference examples, denoted as: Dinfer={(xi, Yi)}=1 (n is 1 or 2).

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: At inference time, only the demo pairs Demo and one input Xinfer ∈ Dinfer are given

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: the model is required to infer the desired output Yinfer.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The training set consists of multiple tasks used to train the model offline (i.e., before a new task is given).

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: training set as: Ttrain={T}=1, where k is the number of tasks (400 in ARC-1).

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: samples in Demo for any T∈ Ttrain can be used for training.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The "inference" samples in the training set, that is, Dinfer for any task T∈ Ttrain, are used for validating the training process only.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The test set is a collection of new tasks, which are not seen during offline training.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: test set as: Ttest={T}=1, with l different test tasks.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: any test task is a "complete" and new task

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: for any T∈ Ttest, there also exists a demo set Demo and the pairs (x, y) in Demo are given to the model at inference time.

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: The model should make use of Demo to infer the output of the given Xinfer for this new task.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: The presence of new (x, y) pairs in Demo at inference time allows to perform test-time training [49, 1, 9, 24]

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: perform test-time training [49, 1, 9, 24]

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: ARC is a collection of many different tasks.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: For each task, a few (e.g., 2-4) demonstration pairs (x, y) are given

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: the model is required to infer the output from Xinfer.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The training set Ttrain is a collection of 400 tasks, which can be used for model training.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The test set Ttest contains 400 new tasks

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the demo pairs of a new task are given only at inference time, based on which the model performs inference on Xinfer.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: formulate reasoning on each task as an image-to-image translation problem.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: frame the problem as per-pixel classification

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: analogous to the semantic segmentation problem [38].

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: learn a neural network fe parameterized by 0.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The network fe takes an image xi as input, conditioned on a task token associated with the task T.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: The task token is represented as a learnable embedding dependent on T.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The output of fe is a grid where each position represents a categorical distribution.

nodes.label: eval_metric
nodes.text: the per-pixel cross-entropy loss [38]

nodes.label: eval_metric
nodes.text: D denotes the per-pixel cross-entropy loss between the ground-truth yi and the network output.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Previous methods on ARC generally operate in the space of discrete-valued tokens, motivated by the design of language models.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: explore native designs developed for vision.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: propose more flexible transformations to represent it in a manner similar to natural images.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: define the concept of a "canvas".

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: A canvas has a predefined and sufficiently large size, e.g., 64×64.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: The raw input is transformed and placed onto this canvas.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: This formulation naturally accommodates translation and scale augmentations, which are common strategies for introducing translation and scale invariance in vision

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: set the background of the canvas to an additional background color, i.e., the (C+1)-th color.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: if we naïvely treat each raw pixel as a token, there would be only C distinct tokens.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: our canvas formulation supports a much larger set of local, patch-level configurations.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: with a patch size of 2×2 (see Fig. 5), a single patch can contain multiple colors and, in principle, has an exponentially large cardinality, O(C2×2).

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: This formulation is important for improving generalization performance.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: The “canvas" concept enables us to flexibly apply translation and scale augmen-tations, which are critical in standard vision models.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: These data augmentations encourage the model to learn un-derlying mappings invariant to geometric transformations grounded in the visual world.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: randomly resize it by an integer scaling ratio s, duplicating each raw pixel into s×s (see Fig. 4, left).

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: This is analogous to nearest-neighbor interpolation in natural im-ages.

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: “colors” in ARC do not cor-respond to real-world colors

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: it is not meaningful to perform other interpolations (such as bilinear).

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: randomly place it on the fixed-size canvas.

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: We ensure all pixels are visibile.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: these visual priors are important for generalization to unseen tasks.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: perform image-to-image translation by a standard vision model.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: By default, we use a ViT [17].

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The principle of ViT is Transformer on patches.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: the input canvas is divided into non-overlapping patches (e.g., 2×2), projected by a linear embedding, added with positional embedding [52], and processed by a stack of Transformer blocks [52].

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: The model has a linear projec-tion layer as the output, which performs per-pixel classifica-tion for each patch.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: unlike natural images where each raw pixel has continuous values, in our case, the raw pixels have discrete values.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: before patchifica-tion, we first map each pixel's discrete index into a learnable continuous-valued embedding.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: patchification can be viewed as a special form of convolution.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: it incorporates sev-eral critical inductive biases in vision: most notably, local-ity (i.e., grouping nearby pixels) and translation invariance (i.e., weight sharing across locations).

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Unlike language data, which is generally modeled as 1D sequences, images are inherently 2D.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: This 2D structure can be lost if we naïvely treat the embedded patches as a 1D sequence.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: explicitly modeling positions in 2D is essential.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: adopt separable 2D positional embed-dings, following [11]: with D channels for positional em-beddings, we use the first half of the channels to embed the horizontal coordinate and the second half to embed the vertical coordinate.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: This can be applied both to additive po-sitional embeddings for encoding absolute positions and to the encoding of relative positions (e.g., RoPE [48]).

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: study the more classical vision-based architecture, i.e., convolutional neural networks [30].

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: adopt the U-Net model [46], a hierarchical convolutional network.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The original U-Net was proposed precisely for the image-to-image translation problem of segmentation [46]

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: making it a natural candidate for the problem we consider.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: The raw input undergoes random scale and translation transformations and is placed on the "canvas" (denoted in gray).

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The ViT architecture in VARC.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: The input is randomly placed on a canvas, which is then treated as a natural image and processed by a standard ViT, conditioned on the task token.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: adopt a two-stage training paradigm to learn the param-eters of the neural network.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: This stage is applied on the entire train-ing set Ttrain.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: It is on all demos Demo for any T∈ Ttrain.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: train one model fe jointly for all k training tasks (e.g., k=400), based on the loss in Eq. (1).

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: All tasks share the same parameters, only except that each task has its own task-conditional token.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: do not use the inference set Dinfer from the training tasks (i.e., T∈ Ttrain) to train the model.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: These sets are used only for validation purposes.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: nearly free to use many views.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: We use 510 random views

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: Predictions from different views are consolidated by majority voting [1].

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Test-time training (TTT).

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Given a single new, unseen task T∈ Ttest from the test set, we perform inference by test-time training.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: At inference time, we are given Demo={(xi, Yi)}m=1 with both input and output accessible; the model is required to make prediction for a given Xinfer in this new task T.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The test-time training followed by inference can be viewed abstractly as a function F(Xinfer | Demo)→ Yinfer.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: We perform test-time training for each new task T independently.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: It has a new task token whose parameters are randomly initialized.

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: As there are very few demo pairs in Demo (e.g., 2 to 4)

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we also perform data augmentation.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: at inference time, the model is initialized from offline training, fine-tuned with test-time training only for the single new task T, and then performs inference on Xinfer.

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: As the new demo pairs in Demo are very few

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.note: e.g., 70 seconds per task on a single GPU
nodes.text: this test-time training process remains reasonably fast

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: After test-time training, we apply fe to Xinfer to obtain the final prediction.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: This process is analogous to the classical recognition problems [29, 38].

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we adopt post-processing strategies inspired by recognition methods.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Single-view inference.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Given Xinfer and a single "view" (i.e., with a given scale and translation), we place Xinfer on the canvas and apply fe to predict the output.

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: Since one output location in the raw grid may be predicted by multi-ple pixels on the canvas (e.g., due to rescaling; see Fig. 5)

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: we aggregate all predictions (from softmax outputs) at this location by average pooling.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Multi-view inference.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: It was a common practice to consolidate the predictions from multiple views (e.g., see AlexNet [29]).

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we adopt multi-view inference

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: to im-prove accuracy

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the views are sampled with different augmentations.

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: As the multi-view inference cost is negli-gible compared with test-time training cost

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: it is virtually nearly free to use many views.

nodes.label: eval_metric
nodes.text: Pass@2 accuracy.

nodes.label: eval_metric
nodes.text: the pass@2 accuracy metric: i.e., two different solutions can be produced for evaluation, and a task is considered correct if one is correct.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: we adopt majority voting in multi-view inference and retain the top-2 most populated output solutions.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Canvas.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the canvas size is 64×64.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: In the case of ViT, the patch size is 2×2, resulting in a sequence length of 322.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: an in-teger scaling ratio is randomly sampled, such that the scaled grid is no larger than the canvas size.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the upper-left corner is randomly sampled under the constraint that the placed image is fully visible.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Offline training.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: We use the standard ARC-1 training set Ttrain for training: it has 400 tasks with 2-4 demo pairs each.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we also expand our training set with the RE-ARC set [22], from which we sam-ple 1,000 additional demo pairs per task.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: our full training set has about 400k sample pairs.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: We apply translation and scale augmentation in offline training.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Test-time training.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Given an unseen task T∈ Ttest, we have 2-4 sample pairs in Demo.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: To make test-time training more feasible

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we also augment the single task T into mul-tiple auxiliary tasks.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: We do this by using standard augmen-tation from existing ARC methods: flip, rotation (by 90°, 180°, or 270°), and color permutation.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: We treat each of these test-time training augmentations as an auxiliary task, each assigned a task embedding.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: We also apply translation and scale augmentation in test-time training

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: under the assumption that all auxiliary tasks are translation and scale invariant

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: During test-time training, the prediction from Xinfer becomes progressively more accurate, with the model finally generating the correct prediction.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Our experiments are primarily conducted on the benchmark of ARC-1 [12].

nodes.label: eval_metric
nodes.text: the pass@2 accuracy (referred to simply as "accuracy” hereafter) in percentage (%).

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: To support pass@2 evaluation

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we adopt multi-view inference.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: We also report final results on ARC-2 [14].

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: We evaluate our model on the ARC-1 evaluation set (i.e., Teval).

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: This set is conceptually a test set (see Fig. 3), but with ground truth available only for computing accuracy.

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: In majority voting, two output grids are considered "consistent” only when they are identical across the entire grid.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: The winner is the grid that is "consistent" with the largest number of other output grids.

nodes.text: We compare variants of ViTs and U-Nets of similar sizes.

nodes.label: intervention

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: observe a degradation

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Patchification.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: A key design principle of our method is to prepare the input as a natural image.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: This enables the expansion of the token set from a very limited size (e.g., 10) to an exponentially large number.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: The entries Fig. 7(d-f) all benefit from this design.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we advance from 1×1 patches on a 32×32 canvas to 2×2 patches on a 64×64 canvas.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: Doing so does not increase the computational cost of the Transformer.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the scaling ratio is fixed as 2x.

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: if we constrain each 2×2 patch to cover only one raw pixel, it becomes equivalent to the 1×1 patch counterpart on the 32×32 canvas.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: to ensure a meaningful compar-ison

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we do not impose this constraint, allowing each 2×2 patch to cover multiple colors.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: This can be interpreted as one-pixel translation augmentation on the canvas.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: the 2×2 patchification leads to a noticeable gain

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: each patch can cover multiple colors (as in natural images), which substan-tially enriches the data space for learning.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Translation and scale augmentation.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: even highly capable network architectures still benefit greatly from translation and scale augmentations.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: We draw similar observations in ARC.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we apply fully flexible translation augmen-tation on the canvas.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: this setting yields an additional gain

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we further ap-ply the scale augmentation enabled by the concept of can-vas.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: Scale augmentation yields a substantial gain

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Unlike translation invariance, which can be partially addressed by patchification (i.e., a special form of convo-lution), the ViT architecture has little to no inductive bias about scale invariance.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: This can explain why scale augmen-tation yields a substantial gain.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: ViTs with different width (x-axis) and depth.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: ViT vs. U-Net.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we compare ViT with U-Nets, a type of convolutional network.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: We evaluate three model sizes for each architecture.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: ViTs consistently per-form better

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: all U-Net variants achieve decent accuracy

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: this problem can also be effectively addressed by classical vision backbones.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Scalability.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we show ViTs with varying depths and widths.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: our method demonstrates good scalability: increasing depth and/or width leads to higher accuracy

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: as a result of better fitting.

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: Going beyond this regime can lead to overfitting in our current setting

nodes.label: context

nodes.label: pattern

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Test-time training (TTT) strategies.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we study TTT with and without offline training, and TTT performed jointly on all test tasks vs. independently for each test task.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: offline training greatly improves the per-formance of TTT

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: common sense about the visual world can be learned from the training set.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: even without offline training, our TTT strategy can achieve nontrivial accuracy

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: some tasks in this benchmark can be solved tabula rasa.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: This result outperforms that in [36]

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: under a similar setting.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: performing TTT independently for each test task yields substantially better performance than doing so jointly across all test tasks

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: the latter relies on a stronger assumption about the availability of multiple test tasks at once.

nodes.label: context

nodes.label: context

nodes.label: intervention

nodes.label: assumption

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: System-level Comparisons.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we compare with leading results using LLMs or recurrent models, on ARC-1 and ARC-2.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: Our model compares favorably with some of the most powerful LLMs at the time their results were reported: in-cluding Deepseek, Claude, o3, and GPT-5

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: LLMs are pre-trained on internet-scale data, and some may also incor-porate multimodal data that include images.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Our method does not rely on such data

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: uses a model that is several orders of magnitude smaller.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: In the controlled setting of training from scratch on ARC data

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: our method substantially outperforms the recur-rent models: HRM [53] and TRM [27].

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: Our VARC with 18M parameters is better than TRM on ARC-1

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: once test-time training is completed, our model performs fully feedfor-ward inference, with no recurrence involved in reasoning.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we ensemble one ViT and one U-Net, each with test-time training run four times.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: Doing so boosts our result

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: This result closes the gap with the re-ported average human performance

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: LLM-based results are from the ARC-AGI leader-board [18].

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: HRM, TRM, and our VARC are trained from scratch only on ARC data.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Our single-model result is based on ViT, with mean±std of 54.5±0.7 (ARC-1) and 8.3±0.4 (ARC-2) over four runs.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Our ensemble result aggregates an 18M ViT and a 55M U-Net, each with test-time training performed four times.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Visualization and Analysis.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: we provide additional qualita-tive results that help reveal the model's behavior.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Attention patterns.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Fig. 10 shows the attention patterns of our ViT model in a test task.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: These attention maps show that our model can correctly reason about the relationship between a source pixel and its target pixel to copy from.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Figure 11 visualizes the layer-wise attention maps for an-other test task.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: A layer-wise map is the softmax attention map averaged across all pixels in the layer

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: it reveals which pixels receive the most attention in that layer.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: some layers exhibit strong attention to the 3 × 3 neighborhood

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: reflecting the influence of the pattern's core.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: some other layers (e.g., layers 7-9) focus on the outward-radiating rays

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: corresponding to the rule that extends colored pixels along the eight directions.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: different layers exhibit different specialties: some layers at-tend to the pixels that are to be copied, and some layers attend to the target lines alone the eight directions.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: t-SNE of task embeddings.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Our model is conditioned on a task token, with an embedding learned to represent each task.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: With 400 training tasks in ARC-1, our model learns 400 distinct task embeddings in offline training.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: We visu-alize these 400 embeddings in the 2D space by t-SNE [39]

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Each point corresponds to a task..

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: nearby points in the task embedding space exhibit similar semantics.

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: the top-left corner in Fig. 12 shows two tasks related to coloring; the bottom-left corner shows two tasks related to generalized logic operations (i.e., AND/OR/XOR).

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: This vi-sualization suggests that our method attempts to learn the relations between different tasks

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: which is an essential abil-ity for abstraction and reasoning.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Conclusion.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Our work explores a previously overlooked perspective in the ARC task by framing it as an image-to-image translation problem.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: It naturally enables the adaptation of visual frame-works

nodes.label: pattern
nodes.text: yields strong few-shot generalization competi-tive with recent approaches

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: while remaining orders of mag-nitude smaller than most LLM-based models.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: This opens up a new possibility of treating ARC as a vision-centric prob-lem, emphasizing abstraction and reasoning emerging di-rectly from image pixels.

nodes.label: assumption
nodes.text: it cannot be assumed that multiple unseen tasks will be presented all at once.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Our ARC-2 models are trained only on the ARC-1 dataset, with test-time training and inference on the ARC-2 set.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The gray pixels denote the background tokens [BG], which keep the canvas size fixed (64×64 by default).

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The white pixels denote the border tokens [BD], which indicate the output shape.

nodes.label: context

nodes.text: a pair (x, y) with a scaling ratio of 2x

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: During test-time training

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we augment the single test task T into multiple auxiliary tasks.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: not all of these augmentations correspond to the same underlying rule (e.g., consider "gravity" under a 90° rotation)

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: We use a distinct task embedding for each auxiliary task

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: We apply 2 flippings (horizontal and vertical) or 3 rotations (in multiples of 90°), and 10 predefined color index permutations, resulting in (2+3)×10=50 auxiliary tasks with the original task.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: test-time training for one test task T (assuming 3 raw samples in this task)

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: We train for 100 epochs on these 51 tasks, covering 100 x 51 x 3 = 15.3k samples in total

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Unlike standard semantic segmentation, in ARC, the raw input and output sizes are not always identical (e.g., see Fig. 3, Test Set, Task 1).

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: This issue can be addressed on the canvas in a unified framework.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: In our method, the input/output canvas always has a fixed size and is filled with a background token [BG].

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: when the raw output is placed on the canvas (serving as the ground truth during training)

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we always use an extra border token, [BD], to indicate the right and bottom edges.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: the token [BD] is filled along the one-pixel-wide edge on the right and bottom sides.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: During inference

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we locate the rightmost and bottommost [BD] tokens and crop the output accordingly to recover the final predicted shape.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the number of background pixels [BG] can dominate in some examples

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: we apply attention masks in the self-attention blocks

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: to encourage the model to focus on the foreground pixels.

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: The attention masks are applied after the query-key dot-product computation, adding a large negative value to the keys corresponding to background inputs.

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: The resulting softmax attention scores are therefore zero at those key positions.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: during training

nodes.label: intervention
nodes.text: the loss is computed only on locations where the inputs are not background pixels [BG].

nodes.label: mechanism
nodes.text: These designs encourage the model to pay more attention to foregrounds and therefore improve accuracy
